metadata.summary: Extremely small experiments (hours to days) that validate one assumption before engineering. Pre-register numeric success criteria before launching to isolate falsifiable, high-impact hypotheses.

content.title: Micro-MVPs
content.summary: Micro-MVPs are extremely small, high-signal experiments (hours to days) that validate one specific assumption before committing engineering time. The rule: pre-register the success criterion as a number before launching; if a "micro" MVP takes longer than one week to build, it is not micro.

why.p: Traditional MVPs take weeks or months to build, wasting runway when the core assumption is wrong. Micro-MVPs isolate the riskiest assumption and test it at minimal cost — a landing page tests demand, a Stripe Payment Link tests willingness-to-pay, a Wizard-of-Oz tests workflow fit — each costing hours rather than weeks.

list.item: Validating one specific assumption (demand, willingness-to-pay, workflow fit) before committing engineering time
Pre-PMF stage where every shipped feature is a learning bet, not a delivery
Validating a new segment for an existing product (fake-door, smoke-test)
Solo or 2-person team with limited capacity — micro-MVPs preserve runway
Founder is "in love with a feature" — a micro-MVP is the cheapest way to disprove the hypothesis
Post-PMF feature work for an established product where users expect polish — fake-door damages trust
Compliance, security, or infrastructure work — there is no demand to validate
Teams with no monitoring/instrumentation — you cannot learn from the experiment without measurement
Brand-sensitive launches where being seen running an experiment is reputational risk
B2B enterprise sales cycles where a "video demo" is table stakes, not a validation experiment

section.name: experiment-types
section.p: Six micro-MVP types with effort, what each validates, and the conditions under which each is the right choice.

rule.statement: Landing page (1 day): validates demand and messaging. Use when the core question is "do people want this?" Measure: signup or waitlist conversion rate against a pre-registered threshold.
rule.rationale: A landing page with a CTA isolates demand signal from product quality signal. The Dropbox 3-minute video generated a 75K waitlist from 5K overnight at cost of video production time only.

rule.statement: Concierge (1 week): validates workflow fit and value delivery. Use when the question is "does this process actually help users?" A human manually delivers the service. Measure: task completion rate, willingness to return.
rule.rationale: Concierge MVPs reveal process failures before automating them. Automating a broken workflow is more expensive than discovering it is broken during manual delivery.

rule.statement: Wizard of Oz (days): validates UX and willingness to pay. The user sees a polished interface; humans operate the backend manually. Limit to approximately 50 concurrent users before manual operation collapses.
rule.rationale: Decouples UX validation from automation cost. Answers "would users pay for this experience?" without building the engine.

rule.statement: Video demo (hours): validates interest and virality. Use for consumer products where the value is immediately demonstrable. Measure: shares, comment sentiment, waitlist signups.
rule.rationale: Video is the cheapest signal for "does this concept resonate?" not "does this work?" Do not use as a willingness-to-pay signal.

rule.statement: Fake door (hours): validates feature demand. Show a button or menu item for a non-existent feature. Measure: click-through rate. Requires ethical unwind: inform users the feature does not exist yet.
rule.rationale: Fake doors measure intent, not interest. A user who clicks "Export to CSV" intended to use it. Plan the ethical unwind before launch — users who discover the deception lose trust.

rule.statement: Smoke test / Stripe Payment Link (days): validates price sensitivity and willingness to pay. Show a pricing page with a real checkout. The only reliable willingness-to-pay signal is the credit-card moment.
rule.rationale: Self-reported willingness to pay (surveys) overstates actual behavior by 3-5x. A Stripe checkout with real money is the only honest signal.

example.title: Dropbox video MVP
example.note: Dropbox assumed people wanted easy file sync. Micro-MVP: 3-minute video explaining the concept. Result: waitlist from 5K to 75K overnight. Cost: only video production time. Validated demand before writing a single line of sync code.

example.title: Concierge at scale
example.note: Running a concierge MVP beyond 50 concurrent users without automating anything. The team discovers it is now operating a service business, not validating a product hypothesis.

section.name: process
section.p: Sequential steps from identifying the riskiest assumption to the pivot/persevere/next-assumption decision.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Identify the riskiest assumption. Rank assumptions by: (a) falsifiability — can it be tested cheaply? (b) impact if wrong — would the product fail? The riskiest assumption is high impact AND currently unverified.
rule.rationale: Testing a low-impact assumption cheaply wastes a week. Testing an unfalsifiable assumption wastes indefinitely. The intersection of high-impact and falsifiable is where micro-MVPs produce the best ROI.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Design the smallest experiment to test that specific assumption. One assumption per experiment. Cap experiment cost at one week of work. If building it takes longer, decompose further.
rule.rationale: Compound experiments yield ambiguous results. If the hypothesis is wrong, you cannot identify which assumption failed. Single-assumption experiments produce clean decisions.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Pre-register the success criterion before building anything. Define the metric, threshold, minimum sample size, and traffic source. Commit this in a file or document before any traffic touches the asset.
rule.rationale: Deciding the threshold after seeing the data is post-hoc rationalization. Pre-registration forces honesty about what "success" means before survivorship bias sets in.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Run the experiment. Duration: hours to days. Plan the ethical unwind before launch, especially for fake-door experiments collecting user data.
rule.rationale: The ethical unwind (what happens to signups if the product is not built) must be planned before launch, not after. A 200-email waitlist with no follow-up is a trust violation.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Analyze results against the pre-registered criterion. Allow at most one extension of up to 7 additional days if the sample size is insufficient. After one extension, make a decision on available data.
rule.rationale: Indefinite extensions are equivalent to not having a success criterion. The hard cap forces a decision even under uncertainty, which is the point of the experiment.

rule.statement: Step 6 — Decide: pivot (assumption was wrong, try a different approach), persevere (assumption validated, build), or move to next assumption (this one is not the bottleneck). After three failed micro-MVPs on the same problem space, drop the problem space.
rule.rationale: Three failed experiments on a problem space is evidence the problem is not worth solving, not that the experiments are poorly designed. Sunk-cost bias keeps teams iterating past the signal.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Failure modes that produce invalid learning or waste resources, with root causes.

antipattern.description: Validation theatre: a beautifully designed landing page with no distribution plan. The page converts at 0.1% from cold ads but the team declares "validated."
antipattern.reason: The traffic source determines the signal quality more than the asset does. The same page hits 10% on Hacker News Show HN and 0.5% on cold ads. Require an explicit traffic source in the pre-registration before any asset is built.

antipattern.description: Compound experiments: testing two assumptions simultaneously (e.g. demand AND price sensitivity on the same landing page).
antipattern.reason: When the hypothesis is wrong, you cannot identify which assumption failed. Single-assumption experiments produce actionable decisions; compound experiments produce ambiguous data that justifies continuing.

antipattern.description: Post-hoc threshold setting: deciding whether the experiment "succeeded" after seeing the conversion data.
antipattern.reason: Survivorship bias makes any result look like it was close to success. Pre-registration removes the ability to move the goalposts after seeing the outcome.

antipattern.description: Inflated asset bundle: building case studies, customer logos, and testimonials for a one-week experiment.
antipattern.reason: A micro-MVP needs a headline, sub-headline, three value-prop bullets, and a CTA. Additional content delays launch and adds signal noise. Cap copy explicitly.

antipattern.description: Fake-door experiment with no ethical unwind plan. Users click "Get Early Access" and receive no follow-up.
antipattern.reason: Trust violation. Transparency is required: inform users the feature does not exist yet and offer them something real (a waitlist, a refund, a free trial). Plan this before launch.

antipattern.description: Declaring "inconclusive, extend" after every failed experiment. Maximum one 7-day extension; then make a decision on available data.
antipattern.reason: Indefinite extensions are equivalent to having no success criterion. The purpose of the experiment is to produce a decision under uncertainty, not to wait for certainty.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: How autonomous agents integrate micro-MVP methodologies into product workflows.

A hypothesis-extraction agent reads the product brief and surfaces the riskiest unverified assumption with a one-line falsifiability statement. An experiment-design agent picks the cheapest micro-MVP type (landing / video / fake-door / Wizard-of-Oz / concierge / smoke-test) matched to that assumption, plus a numeric success criterion set before launch. An asset-build agent generates the landing copy / fake-door UI / video script. After launch, a measurement agent reads analytics and answers a single question: "did the criterion clear?" Human owns the pivot/persevere/next-assumption call.

Recommended subagents: A hypothesis-extractor agent (Opus) — strategy work; ranking risky assumptions is high-leverage. A copy/asset-build agent (Sonnet) — writes the landing / video script / FAQ for the experiment. An analytics-reader agent — connects to Plausible / Mixpanel / GA4 and reports against the pre-registered criterion. faion-mlp-impl-planner-agent — once an experiment validates, plans the real build.

Prompt pattern for hypothesis extraction: "Product brief: <brief>. List 3-5 unverified assumptions ranked by: Falsifiability (can we test it cheaply?) Impact if wrong (would the product fail?) Output: ranked list, with the riskiest at top. For #1, propose 2 candidate experiments (type, cost in days, success criterion). The success criterion must be a number set BEFORE running the experiment."

Prompt pattern for asset generation: "For experiment <name> with success criterion <metric>=<threshold>: Generate the minimum viable assets: Headline (max 12 words) Sub-headline (one sentence stating the outcome, not the feature) 3-bullet value prop Single CTA copy Reject any output that names internal features rather than user outcomes."

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Core principles for running successful micro-MVP experiments.

rule.statement: Pre-register the success criterion. If you decide the threshold after seeing the data, you are post-hoc rationalizing.
rule.rationale: Pre-registration removes bias and prevents goalpost-moving.

rule.statement: Cap experiment cost at 1 week. If a "micro" MVP takes longer, it is not micro.
rule.rationale: Extended timelines undermine the core value proposition of rapid validation.

rule.statement: Plan the ethical unwind before launch — what happens to the 200 emails on a fake-door waitlist?
rule.rationale: Ethical planning prevents trust violations and reputational damage.

rule.statement: One assumption per experiment. Compound experiments yield ambiguous results.
The traffic source matters more than the asset. Same landing page hits 10% on Hacker News and 0.5% on cold ads.
rule.rationale: Distribution and audience quality dominate asset quality in determining signal.

rule.statement: Smoke-test pricing with a Stripe Payment Link before building checkout — the "credit-card moment" is the only reliable willingness-to-pay signal.
rule.rationale: Real payment behavior trumps self-reported intention surveys by 3-5x.

rule.statement: After 3 failed micro-MVPs on the same problem space, drop the problem space, not the experiment design.
rule.rationale: Repeated failure on the same problem signals the problem is not worth solving, not that the methodology is broken.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when LLMs design or execute micro-MVPs.

antipattern.description: LLMs ship "validation theatre" — beautifully written landing pages with no real distribution plan. Force the prompt to include a traffic-source section.
antipattern.reason: Language models optimize for coherent prose, not distribution strategy. Without explicit prompting, they produce polished but untestable outputs.

antipattern.description: Models hallucinate plausible conversion-rate benchmarks ("industry standard is 8%"). Require citation or omit; otherwise the success criterion is anchored on fiction.
antipattern.reason: Plausible-sounding numbers get cached into decision-making even when unsourced.

antipattern.description: Agents will inflate the asset bundle (case studies, logos, testimonials) for a 1-week experiment. Cap copy length explicitly.
antipattern.reason: Without constraints, models default to comprehensive design, not minimal validation.

antipattern.description: Post-experiment, agents have a strong bias to declare "inconclusive, extend." Force the decision rule with a hard cap (e.g. max 1 extension).
antipattern.reason: Models optimize for narrative coherence; inconclusive results generate more coherent extended narratives than hard decisions.

antipattern.description: Human-in-loop checkpoint: pre-registration commit before any traffic touches the asset. Agents can draft, humans must approve.
antipattern.reason: Pre-registration integrity requires human sign-off; agents cannot bind themselves to thresholds.

antipattern.description: Human-in-loop checkpoint: ethical unwind plan must be human-signed, especially for fake-door experiments that collect data.
Do not let the agent run the post-mortem alone — it tends to weave a coherent narrative regardless of result, masking learning.
antipattern.reason: Models prioritize narrative closure over accurate causal inference.

template.purpose: Pre-registration card: hypothesis, type, success criterion, ethical unwind, decision rule
